_meta.note: Stances reflect what THIS paper experimentally demonstrates. Discussion-section 'theoretical advantages' are tagged basis=theoretical with neutral stance, not 'supports'.

document.title: Genetic engineering approach to develop next-generation reagents for endotoxin quantification

authors.name: Hikaru Mizumura
authors.affiliation: LAL Research and Development Department, Seikagaku Corporation, Tokyo, Japan

authors.name: Toshio Oda
authors.affiliation: LAL Research and Development Department, Seikagaku Corporation, Tokyo, Japan

institutions.name: Seikagaku Corporation
institutions.note: manufacturer of Endospecy ES-50M and the RCR; 6 of 7 authors employed here

institutions.name: Kyushu University
institutions.note: Department of Biology, Faculty of Science

methods.name: SDS-PAGE + Western blotting
methods.params: +/- reducing DTT; PVDF; Abs 2C12 (factor C H-chain), alpha-FCL (FC L-chain), alpha-FB, alpha-PCE; HRP secondary; luminescent detection

methods.name: Recombinant baculovirus transient expression (Sf9)
methods.params: vector pPSC8; Sf9; MOI 0.5-1.0; 48-96 h; leupeptin + pepstatin A tested

methods.name: Sf9 stable expression system
methods.params: vector pIZ-V5; Zeocin selection; colony-formation derivation; used for CSF, rFB, rPCE

methods.name: CHO DG44 stable expression system
methods.params: vector pCI-neo + DHFR; geneticin then stepwise methotrexate amplification; chemically defined suspension medium

methods.name: Factor C purification (chromatography + gel filtration)
methods.params: dextran sulfate-Sepharose CL-6B, eluted 20 mM Tris-HCl pH 8.0 + 450 mM NaCl; gel filtration

methods.name: Chromogenic cascade-reconstruction / endotoxin quantitation assay
methods.params: 100 mM Tris-HCl pH 8.0; zymogens 120-240 ug/ml; 50 ul sample + 50 ul enzyme; Wellreader MP-96, 37C; A405 (ref 492), every 15 s for 30-60 min; mAbs/min; substrates Boc-LGR-pNA (clotting enzyme), Boc-VPR-pNA (factor B), Boc-MTR-pNA (proclotting enzyme); includes the seven 1/2/3-zymogen combination experiments; USP RSE

methods.name: Glycopeptidase F deglycosylation
methods.params: glycopeptidase F; 14 h + DTT at 37C; N-glycan removal; size change by Western blot

methods.name: Lectin-binding dot-blot analysis
methods.params: four factor C + fetuin control on nitrocellulose; biotinylated lectins + HRP-streptavidin. Con A (alpha-Man), LCA (Fuc), MAM (alpha-2,3 sialic acid), SSA (alpha-2,6 sialic acid)

methods.name: Injectable-drug interference assay
methods.params: four RCRs vs eight endotoxin-spiked injectable drugs; residual protease activity as % of water-for-injection baseline; sodium citrate among drugs

methods.name: Standard-curve / sensitivity comparison
methods.params: RCR (CCHO + Sf9 FB + Sf9 PCE) vs natural lysate Endospecy ES-50M; standard-curve slope comparison

preparations.name: Natural factor C from Tachypleus tridentatus lysate
Recombinant factor C from CHO DG44
preparations.note: mammalian; largest apparent MW; MAM+ (alpha-2,3), SSA-; selected for final RCR

preparations.name: Recombinant factor C from Sf9
preparations.note: insect; smallest apparent MW; MAM-, SSA- (no terminal sialic acid)

preparations.name: Recombinant cascade reagent
preparations.note: final = CCHO + Sf9 FB + Sf9 PCE

claims.text: In baculovirus transient expression, factor C and proclotting enzyme bands appeared at 48 h but were replaced by smaller degradation bands at >=72 h.
claims.quote: after 72 h or more, the bands observed at 48 h disappeared, and several smaller bands appeared

claims.text: The baculovirus-system degradation was NOT COMPLETELY suppressed by leupeptin and pepstatin A (partial suppression only).
claims.quote: The degradation was not completely suppressed by the addition of protease inhibitors (leupeptin and pepstatin A...)
claims._revised: wording softened from 'not suppressed' per verifier

claims.text: The Sf9 stable system avoided the time-dependent degradation seen with baculovirus and gave higher factor C yield.
claims.quote: Unlike the baculovirus method, the target zymogens were not degraded over time in culture.

claims.text: Protease activity rose progressively with the number of zymogens — barely with factor C alone, slightly with FC+FB, and rapidly with all three; FULL amplification requires all three, but partial activity exists with fewer (not strictly zero).
claims.quote: the protease activity hardly increased [A] ... increased to a slightly greater extent [B] ... increased rapidly and reached a plateau [C]
claims._revised: stance supports->qualifies; 'only when all three' overstated A/B as zero per verifier

claims.text: Combination A (factor C + Boc-VPR-pNA) showed barely any protease-activity increase.
claims.quote: the protease activity hardly increased

claims.text: Combination B (FC+FB + Boc-MTR-pNA) increased activity slightly more than A.
claims.quote: increased to a slightly greater extent than that observed for combination A

claims.text: Combination C (full cascade FC+FB+PCE + Boc-LGR-pNA) increased activity rapidly, plateauing after ~15 min.
claims.quote: increased rapidly and reached a plateau after approximately 15 min of incubation

claims.text: The three-zymogen cascade greatly amplified protease ACTIVITY relative to recombinant factor C alone; limited proteolysis of all three zymogens confirmed by blot.
claims.quote: the protease activity was greatly enhanced in the presence of the three recombinant zymogens compared with that of recombinant factor C alone

claims.text: Apparent MW of factor C (H+L, non-reducing) ranked CCHO > CTAL > CHEK > CSF.
claims.quote: The largest recombinant factor C protein (H + L chains) under non-reducing conditions was CCHO, followed by CTAL, CHEK and CSF.

claims.text: After glycopeptidase F the H chains shrank and equalized across preparations; the CSF L chain remained slightly larger.
claims.quote: The H chains of CTAL, CCHO, CHEK and CSF displayed reduced sizes ... and were equivalent in size among the treated samples.

claims.text: Con A (alpha-Man) bound all four factor C preparations and fetuin.
claims.quote: Con A ... bound to fetuin ... and to CTAL, CCHO, CHEK and CSF

claims.text: LCA (Fuc) bound the factor C preparations and fetuin.
claims.quote: LCA, a Fuc-specific lectin
claims._note: per-lane detail less explicit in text than Con A

claims.text: MAM (alpha-2,3 sialic acid) bound strongly to CCHO then CHEK, slightly to CTAL and fetuin, and not to CSF.
claims.quote: MAM ... bound strongly to CCHO and CHEK in decreasing order, slightly to CTAL and fetuin, and not to CSF

claims.text: SSA (alpha-2,6 sialic acid) bound strongly to CTAL, CHEK and fetuin but not to CCHO or CSF.
claims.quote: SSA ... bound strongly to CTAL, CHEK and fetuin but did not bind to CCHO or CSF

claims.text: Terminal-sialic-acid N-glycosylation was present in mammalian CCHO and CHEK but absent in insect CSF.
claims.quote: N-glycosylation containing terminal sialic acids were present in CCHO and CHEK prepared using mammalian cells but not in CSF prepared using insect cells

claims.text: Assay conditions were tuned so the four RCRs detected endotoxin equivalently in water for injection at baseline.
claims.quote: we determined the conditions under which the four RCRs exhibited an equivalent ability to detect endotoxin in water for injection

claims.text: RCRs with mammalian CCHO and CHEK were less susceptible to injectable-drug interference than CSF and CTAL; CCHO had the greatest residual activity except with sodium citrate.
claims.quote: RCRs containing CCHO and CHEK (mammalian cells) were less susceptible to interference than those containing CSF and CTAL; furthermore, CCHO exhibited the greatest residual protease activity, except when sodium citrate was used

claims.text: The RCR showed greater endotoxin sensitivity than natural lysate ES-50M, evidenced by a steeper standard-curve slope.
claims.quote: The RCR displayed greater sensitivity to endotoxin than did the natural lysate reagent, as evidenced by the steeper slope of the RCR standard curve
claims._note: single-curve comparison; confidence lowered from 0.9 per verifier

claims.text: The recombinant cascade reagent is a viable animal-free substitute for natural LAL/TAL lysate reagents, usable on existing instrumentation.
claims.quote: The RCR may be considered a promising substitute for natural lysate reagents because it requires only the tools and instruments that are normally used with the natural lysate reagent.

claims.text: The three-enzyme cascade amplifies protease ACTIVITY relative to single recombinant factor C.
claims.quote: this cascade was efficient for signal amplification
claims._revised: DROPPED 'more sensitive than single rFC' (never tested) and split out the 'more potent than natural lysate' theoretical claim -> NC7, per verifier

claims.text: The RCR should avoid beta-glucan false positives because it lacks factor G.
claims.quote: beta-Glucan-induced false positivity does not occur because the RCR does not contain factor G
under: theoretical advantage — NOT experimentally tested in this study
claims._revised: stance supports->neutral; Discussion 'theoretical advantage', no factor-G experiment performed

claims.text: The chromogenic RCR should have no coagulogen competition because coagulogen is absent.
claims.quote: No competition occurs between the synthetic substrate and naturally existing substrate, coagulogen
under: theoretical advantage — NOT experimentally tested in this study

claims.text: CCHO (CHO DG44) was selected as the most promising factor C owing to reduced drug interference; the authors PRESUME this may relate to mammalian terminal sialylation but say further study is needed.
claims.quote: we found that CCHO is the more promising form of factor C than CSF owing to its reduced susceptibility to interference ... We presume that such additions may contribute ... but further studies are necessary

claims.text: Batch-to-batch sensitivity fluctuations of natural lysates should be reduced in the RCR system (recombinant, defined components).
claims.quote: Batch-to-batch fluctuations in the sensitivity of natural lysates ... should be reduced in the RCR system
under: theoretical advantage — NOT experimentally tested in this study
claims._added: missing Discussion advantage (4) flagged by verifier

claims.text: The RCR MAY offer more potent protease activity than natural lysate because it lacks the natural protease inhibitors (serpins).
claims.quote: The RCR may offer potent protease activity because it lacks the protease inhibitors
under: theoretical advantage — stated as 'may', NOT measured
claims._added: split from old NC2; theoretical, not a measured result

conditions.value: insect (Sf9) vs mammalian (CHO DG44 / HEK293) vs natural (T. tridentatus)
chromogenic A405 (mAbs/min) vs Western blot vs lectin dot-blot
water for injection vs 8 injectable drugs (incl. sodium citrate)
terminal sialic acid alpha-2,3 (MAM) vs alpha-2,6 (SSA)
no factor G / no beta-glucan reactivity (theoretical)

cites.ref: Kobayashi et al. (factor B binds endotoxin)
Ding & Ho (recombinant factor C history; prior hosts failed, Sf9 succeeded)

conflict_of_interest.declared_by_authors: none ('The author(s) declared no potential conflicts of interest...')
conflict_of_interest.funding: joint research fund of the Seikagaku Corporation to S. Kawabata
conflict_of_interest.structural_coi: 6 of 7 authors employed by Seikagaku; paper favours Seikagaku's own RCR over its comparator (also Seikagaku's ES-50M)
conflict_of_interest.action: down-weight RCR-favouring 'supports' claims (NC1) despite the formal no-COI declaration

figures_accounted.Figure 4: AC9, AC10, AC11, AC12, AC13, AC14, AC15
